---
title: "Pandas Series & DataFrame"
module: "M3 — Data & Tooling"
chapter: 2
difficulty: "beginner"
prereqs:
  - "M3 Ch1 — NumPy & vectorization"
learning_objectives:
  - "Describe a Series as a labelled 1-D column of values"
  - "Describe a DataFrame as a table built from named columns of Series"
  - "Load a CSV and inspect it with .shape, .head, .columns, and .describe"
compute: "browser"
status: "stable"
last_reviewed: "2026-06-29"
est_minutes: 16
resources:
  - ../../lib/grader.py
  - ../../lib/data.py
  - data/penguins.csv
pyodide:
  packages:
    - pandas
review_cards:
  - id: series-vs-dataframe
    q: "What is the difference between a pandas Series and a DataFrame?"
    a: "A Series is a single labelled 1-D column of values; a DataFrame is a 2-D table made of several named columns, each of which is a Series."
  - id: shape-returns
    q: "What does df.shape return?"
    a: "A tuple (rows, columns) — the number of rows first, then the number of columns. For the penguins table it is (344, 8)."
  - id: select-a-column
    q: "What do you get back from df['col']?"
    a: "A Series — the single column named 'col', with its row labels (the index) attached."
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light:
        - cosmo
        - ../../theme/larnix.scss
      dark:
        - darkly
        - ../../theme/larnix-dark.scss
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    include-after-body: ../../theme/_after-body.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---

{{< badge difficulty=beginner >}} {{< badge compute=browser >}} {{< badge status=stable >}}

## The hook

Open a spreadsheet and you already understand the shape of pandas. A spreadsheet
is a **table**: rows of records, columns with names, and a header strip along the
top. Pandas gives you that same table in Python — except now you can sort,
filter, average, and reshape millions of rows with a line of code instead of
dragging a mouse.

Pandas has exactly **two** objects you need to meet first, and everything else
builds on them:

- A **Series** — one column. A labelled list of values, like a single column of
  the spreadsheet pulled out on its own.
- A **DataFrame** — the whole table. A collection of named columns sitting side
  by side, each column being a Series.

That's the entire mental model. In this chapter we load a real dataset — 344
penguins measured at Palmer Station, Antarctica — and learn the handful of
commands that let you size up *any* table the moment it lands on your screen.

> You'll need from before: NumPy arrays and vectorized thinking (M3 Ch1). A
> pandas column is a NumPy array wearing a name tag, so that intuition carries
> straight over.

---

## A Series is a labelled column

A Python list holds values by *position* — `values[0]`, `values[1]`, and so on. A
**Series** holds values too, but it pairs each value with a **label** (its
*index*). By default the labels are `0, 1, 2, …`, but the point is that the
label travels with the value wherever it goes.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "A Series: values on the right, their index labels on the left"
import pandas as pd

masses = pd.Series([3750, 3800, 3250, 3450])
masses
```

Notice the two columns of output: the **index** (`0, 1, 2, 3`) on the left and the
**values** on the right, plus a `dtype` (here `int64`) telling you what kind of
data this column holds. A Series is one-dimensional — a single line of data with
labels.

Because a Series is built on NumPy, whole-column arithmetic and summaries come for
free. There is no loop to write:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Whole-Series operations: a summary and a vectorized transform"
import pandas as pd

masses = pd.Series([3750, 3800, 3250, 3450])
print("mean mass:", masses.mean())
print("in kilograms:")
print(masses / 1000)
```

`masses.mean()` collapses the column to one number; `masses / 1000` returns a new
Series, dividing every value at once while keeping the same labels. That is the
NumPy "operate on the whole array" idea, now with an index attached.

## A DataFrame is a table of Series

Stack several labelled columns side by side, sharing one index, and you have a
**DataFrame** — pandas' name for a table. Each column is a Series; the column
*names* let you ask for the one you want.

You can build a small DataFrame straight from a dictionary, where each key becomes
a column name and each value becomes that column's data:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "A DataFrame built from a dict: keys are column names, values are columns"
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "species": ["Adelie", "Adelie", "Gentoo"],
    "body_mass_g": [3750, 3800, 5000],
})
df
```

Two named columns, one shared index down the left. Real datasets, though, rarely
arrive as hand-typed dictionaries — they arrive as files. So let's load one.

## Loading a CSV and sizing it up

We'll use the **Palmer Penguins** dataset: body measurements for 344 penguins of
three species. The chapter ships the CSV alongside it, and the `load_csv` helper
reads it for you — the same call works in your browser and in our test runner.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Load the penguins CSV into a DataFrame"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
df.shape
```

The first thing to check on any new table is its **shape**. `df.shape` returns a
tuple `(rows, columns)` — here **`(344, 8)`**. That single line tells you the
dataset has 344 records described by 8 columns, before you've looked at a single
value.

Next, peek at the actual rows with `.head()`, which returns the first five by
default:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "df.head() — the first few rows, to eyeball the data"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
df.head()
```

Now you can see what each row *is*: a penguin, with its species, the island it was
seen on, four body measurements, its sex, and the study year. To get the column
names on their own — handy when a table is too wide to read at a glance — ask for
`.columns`:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "df.columns — the names of every column"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
list(df.columns)
```

That returns the eight names:
`species, island, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g,
sex, year`. The `_mm` and `_g` suffixes are a gift from whoever prepared the data —
the units are baked into the names.

### Summarising every numeric column at once

`.describe()` is the fastest way to understand the *numbers* in a table. For every
numeric column it reports the count, mean, standard deviation, min, max, and the
quartiles — a one-line statistical X-ray:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "df.describe() — count, mean, spread, and quartiles per numeric column"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
df.describe()
```

Read down the `body_mass_g` column of that summary and you'll find a mean of about
**4201.75** grams — these penguins average a little over four kilograms. The
`flipper_length_mm` mean lands near **200.92** mm. Note the `count` row: it can be
*lower* than 344 for some columns, because a few penguins have missing
measurements — `.describe()` quietly skips them, which is a detail worth noticing
early.

### Pulling out one column

To work with a single column, index the DataFrame with its name in square
brackets. What comes back is a **Series** — that same labelled column from the
start of the chapter, now sourced from real data:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Selecting one column returns a Series, which you can summarise"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
mass = df["body_mass_g"]
print(type(mass).__name__)
print("mean body mass:", round(mass.mean(), 2))
```

`df["body_mass_g"]` is a Series, so every Series tool applies: `.mean()` gives
**4201.75**, matching what `.describe()` reported. Selecting a column and
summarising it is the bread-and-butter move of exploratory data analysis, and
you'll repeat it constantly.

One more useful summary for a *text* column is `.value_counts()`, which tallies how
often each value appears:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "value_counts() — how many penguins of each species"
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
df["species"].value_counts()
```

That shows **Adelie 152, Gentoo 124, Chinstrap 68** — Adelie is the most common
species in the dataset. With `.shape`, `.head`, `.columns`, `.describe`, column
selection, and `.value_counts`, you can walk up to almost any table and know what
it holds within a minute.

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaways}
### Key Takeaways

1. A **Series** is a labelled 1-D column: values paired with an index, built on
   NumPy so whole-column maths needs no loop.
2. A **DataFrame** is a 2-D table — several named columns (each a Series) sharing
   one index.
3. `df.shape` returns `(rows, columns)`; for penguins it is **(344, 8)**.
   `.head()`, `.columns`, and `.describe()` size up a table fast.
4. Selecting a column with `df['col']` returns a **Series**, so every Series
   summary like `.mean()` applies to it directly.
:::

---

## Practice

Each exercise is auto-graded by `run_tests`, loaded for you from `lib/grader.py`.
The first two use tiny hand-made data so the answers are exact.

### Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢

Build a Series from a list of numbers and return its total as an `int`. Complete
`series_total` by replacing `____`, then press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_series_total
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_series_total
import pandas as pd

def series_total(values):
    """Build a Series from values and return its sum as an int."""
    return ____  # TODO: int(pd.Series(values).sum())

run_tests([
    ("sums", series_total([10, 20, 30]), 60),
    ("one value", series_total([7]), 7),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Wrap the list in `pd.Series(...)`, call `.sum()` on it, and pass the result to
`int(...)` so the return type is a plain integer.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
import pandas as pd

def series_total(values):
    return int(pd.Series(values).sum())
```

`pd.Series(values)` turns the list into a labelled column; `.sum()` adds every
value, and `int(...)` returns it as a plain Python integer.
</details>

### Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡

Build a DataFrame from a dictionary, select one column by name, and return the
**rounded mean** of that column. Complete the marked line.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_column_mean
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_column_mean
import pandas as pd

def column_mean(data, col):
    """Build a DataFrame from a dict and return the rounded mean of one column."""
    return ____  # TODO: round(float(pd.DataFrame(data)[col].mean()), 2)

run_tests([
    ("mean of a", column_mean({"a": [1, 2, 3], "b": [4, 5, 6]}, "a"), 2.0),
    ("mean of b", column_mean({"a": [1, 2, 3], "b": [4, 5, 6]}, "b"), 5.0),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

`pd.DataFrame(data)` builds the table; `[col]` selects one column as a Series;
`.mean()` averages it. Wrap that in `float(...)`, then `round(..., 2)` for two
decimal places.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
import pandas as pd

def column_mean(data, col):
    return round(float(pd.DataFrame(data)[col].mean()), 2)
```

`pd.DataFrame(data)[col]` is the column as a Series; `.mean()` averages it,
`float(...)` makes the type predictable, and `round(..., 2)` trims it to two
decimal places.
</details>

### Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴

Open-ended, **rubric-graded** (not auto-checked). Load the penguins dataset, run
`df.describe()`, and report two facts in your own `print` statements: the **mean
body mass** (about 4201.75 g) and which **species is most common** (Adelie, with
152 birds).

**Rubric — a good answer:**

- Loads penguins with `load_csv` and calls `df.describe()` to inspect the numbers.
- Prints the mean of `body_mass_g` (≈ 4201.75) read from the data, not typed in by
  hand.
- Reports the most common species using `value_counts()` (Adelie, 152).

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Select `df["body_mass_g"]` and call `.mean()` for the first fact. For the second,
`df["species"].value_counts()` lists species by frequency; the first row is the
most common one, and `.idxmax()` gives only its name.
</details>

<details><summary>Show one possible answer</summary>

```python
from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
print(df.describe())

mean_mass = round(df["body_mass_g"].mean(), 2)
print("mean body mass (g):", mean_mass)

counts = df["species"].value_counts()
print("most common species:", counts.idxmax(), "with", int(counts.max()), "birds")
```

This prints a mean body mass of `4201.75` and reports `Adelie` as the most common
species with `152` birds — both read straight from the data rather than typed in.
</details>

---

## Check yourself

{{< quiz quiz-ch02.yml >}}

---

## Where this fits / what's next

You can now load a table and size it up — shape, head, columns, a numeric summary,
and a single column pulled out as a Series. That inspection ritual is the first
thing a working data scientist does with any new dataset. Next we go from *looking*
to *choosing*: selecting rows and columns by label and position, and filtering a
DataFrame down to only the penguins you care about.
